title: Enteric infection induces Lark-mediated intron retention at the 5' end of Drosophila genes
author: Bou Sleiman, Maroun
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Background  

 RNA splicing is a key post-transcriptional mechanism that generates protein diversity and contributes to the fine-tuning of gene expression. This ability to control and diversify the transcriptional output of the genome may facilitate an organism’s adaptation to environmental challenges. Here, we employed a systems approach to study the alternative splicing landscape changes upon enteric infection of the gut in females from classical  Drosophila melanogaster  strains as well as 38 inbred lines. 

  Results  

 We found that infection leads to extensive and consistent differences in isoform ratios, which result in a more diverse transcriptome with longer 5’ untranslated regions (5’ UTRs). We established a role for genetic variation in mediating inter-individual splicing differences, with local splicing Quantitative Trait Loci ( local- sQTLs) being preferentially located at the 5’ end of transcripts and directly upstream of splice donor sites. Moreover,  local- sQTLs were more numerous in the infected state, indicating that acute stress unmasks a substantial number of otherwise silent genetic variants. We also observed a general and consistent increase in intron retention events concentrated at the 5’ end of transcripts across multiple  Drosophila  strains, whose prevalence scaled with the degree of virulence of different pathogens. The length, GC content, and RNA Polymerase II occupancy of these retained introns suggest that they have exon-like characteristics. We further uncovered that the intron sequences that are retained after infection are enriched for the Lark/RBM4 RNA binding motif. Interestingly, we found that  Lark  is induced by infection in wild-type flies, that its overexpression and knockdown alter survival to infection, and that its tissue-specific overexpression mimics infection-induced intron retention. 

  Conclusion  

 Our collective findings point to pervasive and consistent RNA splicing changes, partly mediated by Lark/RBM4, as being an important aspect of the gut response to infection.
